items.passage:
Fluid dynamics, the branch of physics that studies how liquids and gases move, explains the forces that arise as objects travel through a flowing medium. A central principle is that a fluid in motion exerts pressure that varies with its speed: where a fluid flows faster, its pressure drops, and where it slows, pressure rises. This inverse relationship, formalized as Bernoulli's principle, governs phenomena from the lift on a wing to the path of a spinning ball.

This pressure behavior becomes vivid when fluid meets a curved surface. As air streams over the top of a wing, it accelerates and its pressure falls, while the slower air beneath retains higher pressure. The difference pushes the wing upward, generating lift. The same mechanism steers a curving baseball, whose spin drags air faster along one side and bends the ball toward it.

Beyond such steady patterns, real fluids often break into chaotic, swirling motion known as turbulence. When flow speed passes a critical threshold, smooth streamlines collapse into irregular eddies that mix the fluid and increase drag. Turbulence dissipates energy and resists precise calculation, so engineers rely on approximations and wind-tunnel tests. However, no complete theory yet predicts when turbulence emerges, and this gap remains one of the field's deepest unsolved problems.
paragraphs: Fluid dynamics, the branch of physics that studies how liquids and gases move, explains the forces that arise as objects travel through a flowing medium. A central principle is that a fluid in motion exerts pressure that varies with its speed: where a fluid flows faster, its pressure drops, and where it slows, pressure rises. This inverse relationship, formalized as Bernoulli's principle, governs phenomena from the lift on a wing to the path of a spinning ball.

This pressure behavior becomes vivid when fluid meets a curved surface. As air streams over the top of a wing, it accelerates and its pressure falls, while the slower air beneath retains higher pressure. The difference pushes the wing upward, generating lift. The same mechanism steers a curving baseball, whose spin drags air faster along one side and bends the ball toward it.

Beyond such steady patterns, real fluids often break into chaotic, swirling motion known as turbulence. When flow speed passes a critical threshold, smooth streamlines collapse into irregular eddies that mix the fluid and increase drag. Turbulence dissipates energy and resists precise calculation, so engineers rely on approximations and wind-tunnel tests. However, no complete theory yet predicts when turbulence emerges, and this gap remains one of the field's deepest unsolved problems.

questions.stem: The word "varies" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: changes
options.B: travels
options.C: expands
options.D: weakens

questions.explanation: The passage explains that pressure "varies with its speed" — dropping where flow is faster and rising where it slows — so the pressure changes with speed rather than traveling, expanding, or weakening.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens to the air moving over the top of a wing?

options.A: It accelerates and gains pressure as it crosses the wing.
options.B: It moves more slowly than the air beneath the curved wing.
options.C: It pushes the wing downward by raising the pressure above it.
options.D: It speeds up and loses pressure as it passes the wing.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "As air streams over the top of a wing, it accelerates and its pressure falls," which matches the description of air speeding up while losing pressure.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about a spinning baseball?

options.A: A baseball with no spin would curve more sharply in flight.
options.B: Higher pressure always forms on the faster-moving side of a ball.
options.C: A ball spinning faster on one side would deflect more strongly.
options.D: Spinning slows the air equally on both sides of the ball.

questions.explanation: Since spin "drags air faster along one side and bends the ball toward it," a stronger speed difference (faster spin) would lower pressure further on that side, implying a greater deflection.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention a curving baseball in paragraph 2?

options.A: To prove that turbulence has no effect on a moving wing.
options.B: To show the same pressure rule operating in a second case.
options.C: To suggest that baseballs are harder to model than aircraft.
options.D: To question whether Bernoulli's principle applies to sports at all.

questions.explanation: The author introduces the baseball with "The same mechanism steers a curving baseball," using it as a second example of the speed-pressure relationship already shown with the wing.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, all of the following are true of turbulence EXCEPT:

options.A: It collapses smooth streamlines into irregular eddies.
options.B: It tends to increase the drag on a moving object.
options.C: It begins once flow speed passes a critical threshold.
options.D: It can be calculated precisely by current engineering theory.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says turbulence "resists precise calculation" and that "no complete theory yet predicts when turbulence emerges," so the claim that it can be calculated precisely is not supported.

items.passage:
Polymers are large molecules built from many small repeating units, called monomers, linked together in long chains. This simple architecture gives rise to materials as varied as natural rubber, silk, and the synthetic plastics that fill modern life. The length of the chains and the way they are arranged determine whether a polymer is soft and flexible or rigid and tough, making chain structure the key to a material's behavior.

These chains form through a process known as polymerization, in which monomers bond one after another. In addition polymerization, units simply join with no byproduct, while in condensation polymerization each new bond releases a small molecule such as water. The conditions of the reaction control chain length, and longer chains generally yield stronger, more heat-resistant materials because their tangled strands grip one another more firmly.

This control over structure has made polymers central to modern technology. Engineers now design chains for specific tasks: stretchable fibers for clothing, durable casings for electronics, and dissolvable threads for surgical stitches. Some newer polymers even respond to heat or light by changing shape. However, many common plastics resist natural breakdown and persist in the environment for centuries, a drawback that continues to challenge chemists seeking sustainable alternatives.
paragraphs: Polymers are large molecules built from many small repeating units, called monomers, linked together in long chains. This simple architecture gives rise to materials as varied as natural rubber, silk, and the synthetic plastics that fill modern life. The length of the chains and the way they are arranged determine whether a polymer is soft and flexible or rigid and tough, making chain structure the key to a material's behavior.

These chains form through a process known as polymerization, in which monomers bond one after another. In addition polymerization, units simply join with no byproduct, while in condensation polymerization each new bond releases a small molecule such as water. The conditions of the reaction control chain length, and longer chains generally yield stronger, more heat-resistant materials because their tangled strands grip one another more firmly.

This control over structure has made polymers central to modern technology. Engineers now design chains for specific tasks: stretchable fibers for clothing, durable casings for electronics, and dissolvable threads for surgical stitches. Some newer polymers even respond to heat or light by changing shape. However, many common plastics resist natural breakdown and persist in the environment for centuries, a drawback that continues to challenge chemists seeking sustainable alternatives.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: How condensation polymerization releases water as chains form bonds.
options.B: How the structure of polymer chains shapes their properties and uses.
options.C: Why most synthetic plastics fail to break down in the environment.
options.D: How engineers design stretchable polymer fibers for modern clothing.

questions.explanation: The passage moves from defining polymer chains, to how they form, to how their structure is engineered for applications — all framed by the claim that "chain structure" is "the key to a material's behavior."

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what distinguishes condensation polymerization?

options.A: They join with no byproduct released as the long chain forms.
options.B: They always give off a small molecule such as water.
options.C: They each release a small molecule as a new bond forms.
options.D: They link monomers together without forming any chemical bond.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "in condensation polymerization each new bond releases a small molecule such as water," which option C paraphrases; the no-byproduct case describes addition polymerization instead.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to the paragraphs before it?

options.A: It merely restates the chemical reactions described in the previous paragraph.
options.B: It introduces a new class of monomers not mentioned earlier on.
options.C: It openly questions the structural claims made earlier in the passage.
options.D: It applies the structural ideas of earlier paragraphs to real uses.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 opens with "This control over structure has made polymers central to modern technology" and then lists practical applications, applying the structural principles established in paragraphs 1 and 2.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about polymers with shorter chains?

options.A: Shorter chains tend to make weaker, less heat-resistant materials.
options.B: Chain length has no clear effect on a material strength.
options.C: All polymers with long chains dissolve readily in water.
options.D: Natural polymers are always stronger than synthetic ones.

questions.explanation: Since "longer chains generally yield stronger, more heat-resistant materials," it follows that shorter chains would tend to produce weaker, less heat-resistant materials.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what is true of some newer polymers?

options.A: They can alter their shape when exposed to light or heat.
options.B: They dissolve harmlessly within the human body after a few days.
options.C: They break down in nature far faster than older plastics do.
options.D: They are made only from natural rubber and silk.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states that "Some newer polymers even respond to heat or light by changing shape," which option A restates as altering shape when exposed to light or heat.

items.passage:
A trophic cascade is an ecological chain reaction in which predators at the top of a food web indirectly shape the abundance of plants far below them. By controlling the populations of the animals they hunt, top predators release or restrain the species at lower levels, sending ripples through the whole community. Ecologists describe such top-down effects as evidence that the loss of a single predator can reshape an entire landscape.

These cascading effects unfold through a series of linked interactions. When predators decline, the herbivores they once suppressed multiply and graze more heavily, stripping away vegetation that other organisms depend on. The classic illustration comes from sea otters, whose appetite for sea urchins protects the kelp forests that urchins would otherwise devour. Remove the otters, and barren rock replaces a once-thriving underwater forest.

This sensitivity to predators has reshaped how conservationists approach whole ecosystems. Rather than protecting single species in isolation, managers increasingly restore key predators to revive the habitats beneath them, as the return of wolves did in some northern parks. However, cascades do not appear everywhere with equal force, and ecologists still debate which conditions allow a predator's influence to travel so far down the chain.
paragraphs: A trophic cascade is an ecological chain reaction in which predators at the top of a food web indirectly shape the abundance of plants far below them. By controlling the populations of the animals they hunt, top predators release or restrain the species at lower levels, sending ripples through the whole community. Ecologists describe such top-down effects as evidence that the loss of a single predator can reshape an entire landscape.

These cascading effects unfold through a series of linked interactions. When predators decline, the herbivores they once suppressed multiply and graze more heavily, stripping away vegetation that other organisms depend on. The classic illustration comes from sea otters, whose appetite for sea urchins protects the kelp forests that urchins would otherwise devour. Remove the otters, and barren rock replaces a once-thriving underwater forest.

This sensitivity to predators has reshaped how conservationists approach whole ecosystems. Rather than protecting single species in isolation, managers increasingly restore key predators to revive the habitats beneath them, as the return of wolves did in some northern parks. However, cascades do not appear everywhere with equal force, and ecologists still debate which conditions allow a predator's influence to travel so far down the chain.

questions.stem: The word "restrain" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: restrict
options.B: feed
options.C: follow
options.D: count

questions.explanation: The phrase "release or restrain the species at lower levels" contrasts releasing with holding back, so "restrain" means to restrict rather than to feed, follow, or count.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how do sea otters affect kelp forests?

options.A: They graze on kelp that sea urchins would otherwise protect.
options.B: They multiply rapidly whenever predator populations begin to grow.
options.C: They eat sea urchins that would otherwise destroy the kelp.
options.D: They replace barren rock with a thriving underwater forest.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that the otters' "appetite for sea urchins protects the kelp forests that urchins would otherwise devour," meaning otters preserve kelp by eating the urchins.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the removal of top predators?

options.A: Predators harm the plant communities at the base of a web.
options.B: Losing top predators can reduce the plants lower in a web.
options.C: Herbivores rarely change the vegetation around them when undisturbed.
options.D: Kelp forests grow best after sea otters have been removed.

questions.explanation: Because removing predators lets herbivores "multiply and graze more heavily, stripping away vegetation," it follows that losing top predators can reduce the plant life lower in the food web.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention sea otters in paragraph 2?

options.A: To prove that sea urchins are harmful to every ecosystem.
options.B: To question whether otters truly belong in kelp forests at all.
options.C: To give a concrete example of how a cascade unfolds.
options.D: To show that kelp forests recover quickly without any predators.

questions.explanation: The otters are introduced as "The classic illustration," serving as a specific example of the linked interactions through which a trophic cascade operates.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Restoring key predators can help revive the habitats below them.
options.B: Managers once protected single species in isolation from others.
options.C: The return of wolves changed habitats in some northern parks.
options.D: Cascades appear with equal strength in every kind of ecosystem.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states that "cascades do not appear everywhere with equal force," directly contradicting the claim that they appear with equal strength in every ecosystem.

items.passage: The endowment effect is a tendency in behavioral economics for people to value an object more highly simply because they own it. A coffee mug, once given to a person, is suddenly worth more to that owner than the price they would have paid to buy it moments earlier. This pattern departs from the classical economic assumption that an item's worth is fixed and independent of who holds it. Researchers explain the effect through loss aversion, the principle that the pain of giving something up feels stronger than the pleasure of acquiring something of equal value. Because parting with a possession registers as a loss, the owner demands a higher price to surrender it. Ownership thus shifts a person's reference point, recoloring the same object as either a gain or a loss. The endowment effect carries practical weight far beyond the laboratory. Marketers exploit it through free trials, knowing that customers who already possess a product become reluctant to return it. The effect also helps explain why negotiations stall and why investors cling to declining stocks. However, its strength appears to vary with culture and experience, and seasoned traders seem to display it far less.
paragraphs: The endowment effect is a tendency in behavioral economics for people to value an object more highly simply because they own it. A coffee mug, once given to a person, is suddenly worth more to that owner than the price they would have paid to buy it moments earlier. This pattern departs from the classical economic assumption that an item's worth is fixed and independent of who holds it.

Researchers explain the effect through loss aversion, the principle that the pain of giving something up feels stronger than the pleasure of acquiring something of equal value. Because parting with a possession registers as a loss, the owner demands a higher price to surrender it. Ownership thus shifts a person's reference point, recoloring the same object as either a gain or a loss.

The endowment effect carries practical weight far beyond the laboratory. Marketers exploit it through free trials, knowing that customers who already possess a product become reluctant to return it. The effect also helps explain why negotiations stall and why investors cling to declining stocks. However, its strength appears to vary with culture and experience, and seasoned traders seem to display it far less.

questions.stem: The word "surrender" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: relinquish
options.B: purchase
options.C: damage
options.D: conceal

questions.explanation: The owner demands a higher price to part with the possession, so "surrender" means to relinquish or give it up.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why does an owner demand a higher price for a possession?

options.A: Because the object's market value has risen sharply since purchase
options.B: Because giving up the possession is felt as a loss
options.C: Because the owner wishes to discourage all potential buyers
options.D: Because acquiring objects brings more pleasure than keeping them

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that parting with a possession registers as a loss, so the owner demands a higher price to surrender it.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from the passage about classical economic theory?

options.A: It treats ownership as irrelevant to how much an object is worth
options.B: It predicts that owners will always agree to sell objects at a loss
options.C: It was developed mainly to explain the everyday behavior of marketers
options.D: It denies that people experience any real pain when losing possessions

questions.explanation: The passage says the endowment effect departs from the classical assumption that worth is fixed and independent of who holds it, implying classical theory treats ownership as irrelevant to worth.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention free trials in paragraph 3?

options.A: To question whether the endowment effect truly exists at all
options.B: To contrast modern marketing with careful laboratory research methods
options.C: To explain how the principle of loss aversion first came to be measured
options.D: To give a real-world example of the endowment effect in use

questions.explanation: Free trials are offered as an instance of marketers exploiting the endowment effect, a practical example beyond the laboratory.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of the endowment effect EXCEPT:

options.A: It can be observed equally in everyone regardless of background
options.B: It is connected to the principle of loss aversion
options.C: It may influence how investors treat declining stocks
options.D: It causes people to value objects they own more highly

questions.explanation: The passage states the effect's strength appears to vary with culture and experience and is weaker in seasoned traders, contradicting the claim that it appears equally in everyone.

items.passage: The flying buttress is an arched stone support that braces a wall from the outside, transferring the outward thrust of a vaulted roof down to a freestanding pier. This invention, perfected by Gothic builders in twelfth-century France, solved a problem that had long constrained cathedral design. [■] Earlier Romanesque churches relied on thick, solid walls to bear the heavy roof, which left little room for windows and produced dim, fortress-like interiors. By moving the structural load outside the building, the flying buttress allowed walls to become thinner and taller. [■] Masons could now pierce them with vast expanses of glass without fear of collapse. [■] The downward pressure of the vault was caught by the arch, carried across an open span, and grounded in the external pier. [■] This redistribution of force transformed the experience of sacred space. Walls dissolved into towering windows of stained glass, flooding the interior with colored light that medieval worshippers read as a vision of the heavens. The technique enabled the soaring heights of cathedrals such as Chartres and Amiens. However, the slender designs it permitted sometimes proved unstable, and several ambitious vaults collapsed before builders learned the limits of stone.
paragraphs: The flying buttress is an arched stone support that braces a wall from the outside, transferring the outward thrust of a vaulted roof down to a freestanding pier. This invention, perfected by Gothic builders in twelfth-century France, solved a problem that had long constrained cathedral design. [■] Earlier Romanesque churches relied on thick, solid walls to bear the heavy roof, which left little room for windows and produced dim, fortress-like interiors.

By moving the structural load outside the building, the flying buttress allowed walls to become thinner and taller. [■] Masons could now pierce them with vast expanses of glass without fear of collapse. [■] The downward pressure of the vault was caught by the arch, carried across an open span, and grounded in the external pier. [■] This redistribution of force transformed the experience of sacred space.

Walls dissolved into towering windows of stained glass, flooding the interior with colored light that medieval worshippers read as a vision of the heavens. The technique enabled the soaring heights of cathedrals such as Chartres and Amiens. However, the slender designs it permitted sometimes proved unstable, and several ambitious vaults collapsed before builders learned the limits of stone.

questions.stem: The word "thrust" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: decoration
options.B: force
options.C: shadow
options.D: height

questions.explanation: The flying buttress transfers the outward push of the roof down to a pier, so "thrust" means a force.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what was a drawback of Romanesque churches?

options.A: Their roofs were too light to remain stable in wind
options.B: Their thick walls left little space for windows
options.C: Their piers stood too far from the main building
options.D: Their stained glass admitted too much colored light

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says Romanesque churches relied on thick, solid walls that left little room for windows and produced dim interiors.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the walls of a Gothic cathedral?

options.A: They were considerably thicker than those of earlier Romanesque churches
options.B: They carried most of the heavy roof's downward weight all by themselves
options.C: They were decorated only on their outer exterior surfaces
options.D: They no longer needed to bear the full load of the roof

questions.explanation: Because the buttress moved the structural load outside the building, the walls could become thinner and be pierced with glass, implying they no longer bore the full roof load.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention colored light in paragraph 3?

options.A: To argue that the stained glass gradually weakened the cathedral walls
options.B: To show how the new design changed the interior experience
options.C: To explain how medieval masons manufactured panes of stained glass
options.D: To compare French cathedrals with the churches of other regions

questions.explanation: The colored light flooding the interior illustrates how the buttress transformed the experience of sacred space, an effect on the interior rather than a structural claim.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, the cathedral at Chartres replaced nearly its entire upper wall with windows, some rising more than thirteen meters high.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The concrete example of walls replaced by windows best follows the statement that masons could pierce walls with vast expanses of glass (slot 3), which is the first mention of glass windows.

items.passage: Supply chain management coordinates the flow of goods, information, and finances as a product moves from raw materials to the final consumer. This coordination involves many independent firms, each performing a specialized task such as sourcing, manufacturing, or distribution. [■] Because these firms pursue their own profits, their decisions do not always align with the interests of the chain as a whole. [■] A common difficulty is the bullwhip effect, in which small shifts in consumer demand grow into large swings in orders placed upstream. [■] Retailers, fearing shortages, order extra stock; wholesalers then amplify that order; manufacturers, in turn, build excess capacity. The result is wasteful inventory, idle factories, and unstable prices throughout the system. [■] Modern firms attempt to dampen these distortions by sharing real-time sales data across the chain, so that every partner reacts to actual demand rather than to inflated orders. However, such transparency requires trust between competitors and substantial investment in shared technology, conditions that many supply chains still struggle to satisfy.
paragraphs: Supply chain management coordinates the flow of goods, information, and finances as a product moves from raw materials to the final consumer. This coordination involves many independent firms, each performing a specialized task such as sourcing, manufacturing, or distribution. Because these firms pursue their own profits, their decisions do not always align with the interests of the chain as a whole.

A common difficulty is the bullwhip effect, in which small shifts in consumer demand grow into large swings in orders placed upstream. Retailers, fearing shortages, order extra stock; wholesalers then amplify that order; manufacturers, in turn, build excess capacity. The result is wasteful inventory, idle factories, and unstable prices throughout the system.

Modern firms attempt to dampen these distortions by sharing real-time sales data across the chain, so that every partner reacts to actual demand rather than to inflated orders. However, such transparency requires trust between competitors and substantial investment in shared technology, conditions that many supply chains still struggle to satisfy.

questions.stem: The word "dampen" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: reduce
options.B: moisten
options.C: delay
options.D: conceal

questions.explanation: The firms share data to lessen the demand "distortions," so "dampen" means to reduce or weaken them.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens during the bullwhip effect?

options.A: Minor changes in buyer demand become large fluctuations in upstream orders.
options.B: Manufacturers reduce capacity to match falling retail sales.
options.C: Retailers and wholesalers agree to place identical orders.
options.D: Consumer prices remain stable despite shifting inventory.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "small shifts in consumer demand grow into large swings in orders placed upstream."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about the independent firms in a supply chain?

options.A: They are usually owned by a single parent company.
options.B: They may make choices that harm the overall chain.
options.C: They rarely specialize in any particular task.
options.D: They share their profits equally among partners.

questions.explanation: Since firms "pursue their own profits" and decisions "do not always align with the interests of the chain," their choices can work against the whole.

questions.stem: Why does the author describe retailers, wholesalers, and manufacturers in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that manufacturers cause most supply problems
options.B: To compare the profits earned at each stage
options.C: To recommend removing wholesalers from the chain
options.D: To trace how a small demand change is magnified at each stage

questions.explanation: The sequence of retailers, wholesalers, and manufacturers illustrates how an initial order is progressively amplified upstream.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, a brief weekend sales promotion can trigger weeks of overproduction far up the chain.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete example of the bullwhip effect just defined in the sentence before slot 3, and leads into the retailer-wholesaler-manufacturer chain that follows.

items.passage: Public health is the organized effort of a society to prevent disease and prolong life across whole populations rather than individual patients. In the earliest cities, people had no understanding of how illness spread, and waste was simply dumped into the streets and rivers that supplied drinking water. [■] Crowded conditions and contaminated water allowed epidemics of cholera and typhoid to kill thousands within weeks. During the nineteenth century, reformers began mapping outbreaks and noticed that disease clustered around particular water sources. [■] This careful observation led them to suspect a link between dirty water and infection, even before bacteria were identified. Cities responded by building sewers and filtering public water supplies. [■] These sanitary measures dramatically reduced deaths and became the foundation of modern public health systems. Today, agencies track diseases through laboratory testing, vaccination records, and international reporting networks. [■] However, global travel now spreads new infections faster than ever, so even well-funded health systems face outbreaks that no single nation can control alone.
paragraphs: Public health is the organized effort of a society to prevent disease and prolong life across whole populations rather than individual patients. In the earliest cities, people had no understanding of how illness spread, and waste was simply dumped into the streets and rivers that supplied drinking water. Crowded conditions and contaminated water allowed epidemics of cholera and typhoid to kill thousands within weeks.

During the nineteenth century, reformers began mapping outbreaks and noticed that disease clustered around particular water sources. This careful observation led them to suspect a link between dirty water and infection, even before bacteria were identified. Cities responded by building sewers and filtering public water supplies.

These sanitary measures dramatically reduced deaths and became the foundation of modern public health systems. Today, agencies track diseases through laboratory testing, vaccination records, and international reporting networks. However, global travel now spreads new infections faster than ever, so even well-funded health systems face outbreaks that no single nation can control alone.

questions.stem: The word "prolong" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: extend
options.B: measure
options.C: examine
options.D: shorten

questions.explanation: Public health aims to "prevent disease and prolong life," so "prolong" means to extend or lengthen life.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did nineteenth-century reformers notice about disease?

options.A: It spread mainly through direct personal contact.
options.B: It appeared only in the poorest neighborhoods.
options.C: It clustered around particular water sources.
options.D: It disappeared once bacteria were identified.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that reformers "noticed that disease clustered around particular water sources."

questions.stem: What does "This careful observation" refer to in paragraph 2?

options.A: the building of sewers
options.B: the identification of bacteria
options.C: the filtering of water supplies
options.D: the mapping of disease around water sources

questions.explanation: "This careful observation" refers back to reformers mapping outbreaks and noticing that disease clustered around particular water sources.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the reformers' discovery?

options.A: Useful prevention was possible before the cause of disease was fully understood.
options.B: Bacteria had to be identified before any action could be taken.
options.C: Mapping outbreaks proved to be an unreliable method.
options.D: Sewers were built only after vaccines became available.

questions.explanation: Reformers acted on the water-disease link "even before bacteria were identified," implying effective prevention preceded full scientific understanding.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, John Snow traced a deadly London epidemic to a single contaminated public well.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete instance of reformers linking disease to a specific water source, fitting after that observation is introduced at slot 2.

items.passage: A nilometer was a structure used in ancient Egypt to measure the height of the Nile River during its yearly flood. It typically consisted of a staircase or a deep well marked with graduated scales, allowing officials to record exactly how high the floodwaters rose. The device was often placed within temple grounds, where priests controlled access to its readings. Each summer, meltwater and rains far to the south swelled the Nile, which then spilled over its banks and deposited fertile silt across the floodplain. By reading the nilometer, officials could predict whether the coming harvest would be abundant or poor. A high flood promised rich crops, while a low one warned of famine. Because taxes were assessed on expected agricultural output, these measurements directly determined how much grain farmers owed the state. The nilometer thus linked natural cycles to political power, giving rulers a tool for both planning and control. However, the readings could be manipulated, and a priesthood that monopolized this knowledge held considerable influence over ordinary citizens.
paragraphs: A nilometer was a structure used in ancient Egypt to measure the height of the Nile River during its yearly flood. It typically consisted of a staircase or a deep well marked with graduated scales, allowing officials to record exactly how high the floodwaters rose. The device was often placed within temple grounds, where priests controlled access to its readings.

Each summer, meltwater and rains far to the south swelled the Nile, which then spilled over its banks and deposited fertile silt across the floodplain. By reading the nilometer, officials could predict whether the coming harvest would be abundant or poor. A high flood promised rich crops, while a low one warned of famine.

Because taxes were assessed on expected agricultural output, these measurements directly determined how much grain farmers owed the state. The nilometer thus linked natural cycles to political power, giving rulers a tool for both planning and control. However, the readings could be manipulated, and a priesthood that monopolized this knowledge held considerable influence over ordinary citizens.

questions.stem: The word "swelled" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: warmed
options.B: polluted
options.C: narrowed
options.D: enlarged

questions.explanation: The meltwater and rains caused the Nile to grow in volume and overflow its banks, so "swelled" means enlarged.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, where were nilometers often located?

options.A: Within temple grounds controlled by priests
options.B: Along the borders of neighboring kingdoms
options.C: Inside the private homes of wealthy farmers
options.D: At the southern source of the Nile's floods

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states the device "was often placed within temple grounds, where priests controlled access to its readings."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about a low Nile flood?

options.A: It was likely to reduce the size of the harvest.
options.B: It deposited more silt than a high flood.
options.C: It occurred mainly during the winter months.
options.D: It made the nilometer readings more accurate.

questions.explanation: Since a high flood "promised rich crops" while a low one "warned of famine," a low flood implies a reduced harvest.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention that taxes were assessed on expected agricultural output in paragraph 3?

options.A: To criticize the unfairness of the Egyptian tax system
options.B: To compare Egyptian taxes with those of other empires
options.C: To explain how flood measurements gained political importance
options.D: To suggest that farmers frequently refused to pay grain

questions.explanation: Linking taxes to expected output shows why the nilometer's readings mattered politically, connecting natural cycles to state power.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of the nilometer EXCEPT

options.A: It could take the form of a staircase or a deep well.
options.B: It was used to control the direction of the Nile's flow.
options.C: Its readings helped officials predict the harvest.
options.D: Its measurements influenced how much grain farmers owed.

questions.explanation: The nilometer only measured flood height; the passage never claims it controlled the river's flow, while the other options are all stated.
